grammarLessons.name: 있어요 vs 없어요 — Have / Don't Have

cards.grammarNote:
**물** (mul) = water.
**물** also appears in compounds: **물냉면** (cold noodles in broth), **물김치** (watery kimchi).
Ordering water: **물 주세요** (mul juseyo) = 'water please'.
In Korea, restaurants typically bring cold water automatically.

**빵** (ppang) is a loanword from Portuguese *pão* via Japanese.
Korea has a thriving bakery culture — **빵집** (ppangjip) = bakery.
Common types: **식빵** (sikppang) = white sandwich bread, **단팥빵** (danpatppang) = sweet red bean bun.
**빵** is used colloquially to mean 'zero': **빵점** (ppangjeom) = zero points.

**밥** = cooked rice, and also the general word for a meal.
**밥 먹었어요?** (did you eat?) is a common Korean greeting.
**쌀** = uncooked rice.
**밥** is one of the most culturally central words in Korean.

**우유** (uyu) = milk — from Hanja **우** (牛, cow) + **유** (乳, milk).
**두유** (duyu) = soy milk: **두** (豆, bean) + **유** (乳, milk).
**생우유** = fresh/whole milk.
**원하다** (wonhada) = to want (more formal than **-고 싶다** for wanting objects).

**달걀** (dalgyal) is the native Korean word for egg.
**계란** (gyeran) is the Sino-Korean equivalent and equally common in everyday speech.
**두 개** = two (objects) — **개** is the general counter for items.
Time + **에** = 'at': **아침에** = 'at/in the morning'.
cards.englishPhrase: I eat two eggs for breakfast.

cards.grammarNote:
**치즈** (chijeu) is a loanword from English.
**에** after a container or place = 'in / on': **샌드위치에** = 'in the sandwich'.
**가/이** is the subject marker: **치즈가** because **치즈** ends in a vowel.
**있어요** = 'there is / exists' — the same word covers both existence and possession.

**닭고기** (dalkgogi) = chicken (as meat): **닭** (dak, chicken bird) + **고기** (gogi, meat).
Always add **고기** to refer to the meat — **닭** alone means the live bird.
**맛있어요** = 'is delicious' — from **맛** (taste) + **있다** (to exist).
**가** is the subject marker after a vowel: **닭고기가**.

**고기** (gogi) = meat in general.
Specific meats: **소고기** (beef), **돼지고기** (pork), **닭고기** (chicken).
**는** is the topic marker — **그는** = 'he (as for him)'.
**안** before a verb = casual negation: **안 먹어요** = 'doesn't eat'.

**생선** (saengseon) = fish as food (cut, served, or sold at market).
**물고기** (mulgogi) = fish (alive, swimming): **물** (water) + **고기** (flesh/creature).
Use **생선** when buying or eating fish; **물고기** when talking about fish in nature.
**을/를** = object marker: **생선을** because **선** ends in a consonant.

**김치** (gimchi) is Korea's most iconic fermented dish — typically napa cabbage with chili, garlic, ginger, and salted shrimp.
There are hundreds of varieties: **깍두기** (cubed radish), **오이소박이** (cucumber), and more.
**-고** connects adjectives in a list: **맵고 맛있어요** = 'spicy and delicious'.
**는** = topic marker: **김치는** sets kimchi as the topic.

**비빔밥** (bibimbap) = mixed rice: **비빔** (mixing) + **밥** (cooked rice).
Served with vegetables, an egg, and **고추장** (gochujang chili paste) — mix it all together before eating.
**음식** (eumsik) = food — Hanja: **음** (飮, drink/consume) + **식** (食, eat/food).
**은** = topic marker after a consonant: **비빔밥은**.
cards.englishPhrase: Bibimbap is a Korean rice dish.

cards.grammarNote:
**소주** (soju) = Korea's most popular spirit, typically 16–25% ABV, made from rice or other grains.
**술** (sul) = alcohol in general.
**인기 있는** (ingi inneun) = popular: **인기** (popularity) + **있는** (existing/having) — literally 'having popularity'.
Common pairing: **삼겹살에 소주** (pork belly and soju).
cards.englishPhrase: Soju is a popular Korean drink.

cards.grammarNote:
**돼지고기** (dwaejigogi) = pork: **돼지** (pig) + **고기** (meat).
**삼겹살** (samgyeopsal) = pork belly — Korea's most popular BBQ meat.
**는** as a topic marker can hint at contrast: **돼지고기는 안 먹어요** implies 'pork I don't eat (but perhaps other meats I do)'.

**과일** (gwail) = fruit — Hanja root **과** (果, fruit/result).
Fruit is culturally important for gifting in Korea — elaborate fruit gift sets are popular at holidays.
**을** = object marker after a consonant: **과일을**.
**매일** (maeil) = every day — does NOT take the **에** particle (time adverbs stand alone).

**사과** (sagwa) = apple. Importantly, **사과** also means 'apology' — **사과하다** (to apologize). Context always makes the meaning clear.
**건강에 좋다** = 'good for health': **에** here means 'for'.
**하나** = one (native Korean number) — used for counting before counters.
cards.englishPhrase: An apple a day is healthy.

cards.grammarNote:
**바나나** (banana) is a loanword.
**가** = subject marker after a vowel: **바나나가**.
**익다** (ikda) = to ripen (for fruit) OR to cook through (for food) — the same verb covers both.
**익었어요** = 'has ripened' — past tense, indicating a completed change of state.

**오렌지** (orenji) is a loanword from English/Japanese.
The native mandarin-like fruit is **귤** (gyul) — very common in Korea, especially in winter.
**주스** (juseu) = juice (loanword).
**원하다** (wonhada) = to want — used for wanting objects, while **-고 싶다** is used for wanting to do actions.

**채소** (chaeso) = vegetable — the standard, polite term.
**야채** (yachae) is also widely used and means the same thing.
**드세요** = 'please eat' — honorific imperative of **먹다** (to eat). Use **드시다** when speaking respectfully to elders or customers.

**감자** (gamja) = potato.
**고구마** (goguma) = sweet potato — a different, very common Korean snack and side dish.
**으깨다** (eukkkaeda) = to mash/crush.
**으깬** is the past-modifier form: **으깬 감자** = 'mashed potato(es)'.

**토마토** (tomato) is a loanword.
**에** after a container/location = 'in': **샐러드에** = 'in the salad'.
**가** = subject marker after a vowel: **토마토가**.
**있어요** = 'there is/are' — used for existence and possession.

**양파** (yangpa) = onion: **양** (洋, foreign/Western) + **파** (green onion).
**파** alone = green onion/scallion (a separate vegetable).
**를** = object marker after a vowel: **양파를**.
**안** before a verb negates it: **안 좋아해요** = 'don't like'.

**샐러드** (saelleodeu) is a loanword from English.
**로/으로** = 'as / in the form of': **샐러드로 할게요** = 'I'll go with the salad'.
**-ㄹ게요** expresses the speaker's firm decision or commitment: **할게요** = 'I will do/have it'.

Korean has many soup types:
**국** (guk) = lighter everyday soup.
**찌개** (jjigae) = hearty stew, e.g., **김치찌개**.
**탕** (tang) = rich broth-based soup.
All are central to Korean meals.

**샌드위치** (saendeuwichi) is a loanword.
**으로** = 'as / for': **점심으로** = 'for lunch'.
**만들다** (mandeulda) = to make — an irregular ㄹ verb: the ㄹ drops before consonant endings.
**만들었어요** = 'made' — past tense.
cards.englishPhrase: I made a sandwich for lunch.

cards.grammarNote:
**케이크** (keikeu) is a loanword.
For birthdays: **생일 케이크** (birthday cake).
**원해요?** = 'do you want (it)?' — from **원하다** (wonhada, to want).
Dropping the subject is natural in Korean when context is clear.

**쿠키** (kuki) is a loanword from English.
**이** here = demonstrative 'this' — different from the subject marker **이**.
**맛있어요** = 'is delicious': **맛** (taste) + **있다** (to exist/have).
**이 쿠키가** — when **이** (this) precedes a noun, the subject marker **가/이** still follows the noun.

**초콜릿** (chokollit) is a loanword.
**정말** (jeongmal) = 'really / truly' — a common intensifier.
**좋아하다** (joahada) = to like — used for liking things (**을/를 좋아해요**).
Note: **좋다** (jota) = 'to be good', which is different from **좋아하다** (to like).

**설탕** (seoltang) = sugar — Sino-Korean.
**빼 주세요** = 'please leave out / remove': **빼다** (to subtract/remove) + **주세요** (please do for me).
Useful at cafés: **시럽 빼 주세요** (no syrup), **얼음 빼 주세요** (no ice).

**소금** (sogeum) = salt — native Korean.
**짜다** (jjada) = to be salty — an important taste adjective: **너무 짜요** = 'too salty'.
**좀** (jom) softens requests: 'a bit / please'.
**ㄹ 수 있어요** = 'can' (ability/possibility): **건네줄 수 있어요** = 'can you pass it?'

**후추** (huchu) = black pepper.
Do not confuse: **고추** (gochu) = chili pepper, **피망** (pimang) = bell pepper.
**건네줄 수 있어요?** = 'can you pass it?' — **V-ㄹ 수 있다** = 'can / be able to'.
**좀** softens the request slightly.

**버터** (beoteo) is a loanword.
**바르다** (bareuda) = to spread/apply — used for butter, cream, paint, or sunscreen.
**에** after a surface = 'on': **빵에** = 'on the bread'.
**를** = object marker after a vowel: **버터를**.
cards.englishPhrase: I put butter on my bread.

cards.grammarNote:
**기름** (gireum) = oil or fat (general term).
**참기름** (chamgireum) = sesame oil — an essential flavoring in Korean cooking.
**들기름** (deulkireum) = perilla oil — another traditional Korean oil.
**로** = 'with / by means of': **올리브 오일로** = 'with olive oil'.

**커피** (keopi) is a loanword from English.
Korea has one of the highest café densities in the world.
The most popular order is **아이스 아메리카노** (iced Americano).
**에** after a time word = 'in/at': **아침에** = 'in the morning'.
cards.englishPhrase: I like coffee in the morning.

cards.grammarNote:
**차** (cha) = tea — also means 'car' (written with a different Hanja). Context distinguishes them.
Korean teas: **녹차** (green tea), **보리차** (barley tea), **유자차** (yuzu tea).
**드실래요?** = 'would you like (to eat/drink)?' — **드시다** is the honorific of **먹다/마시다**; **-ㄹ래요** asks politely about willingness.

**와인** (wain) is a loanword from English.
**잔** is the counter for cups/glasses of liquid: **한 잔** = one glass.
**주세요** = 'please give me' — the most useful ordering phrase in Korean.
**레드 와인** (red wine), **화이트 와인** (white wine).
cards.englishPhrase: A glass of red wine, please.

cards.grammarNote:
**맥주** (maekju) = beer — Hanja: **맥** (麥, barley/grain) + **주** (酒, alcohol).
**치맥** = **치킨** (fried chicken) + **맥주** — Korea's most iconic food pairing.
**잔** = counter for glasses: **한 잔** (one glass), **두 잔** (two glasses).

**주스** (juseu) is a loanword from English.
**착즙 주스** = cold-pressed/freshly squeezed juice.
**를** = object marker after a vowel: **주스를**.
**원하다** (wonhada) = to want (for wanting things/objects).

**아침** (achim) = morning AND breakfast — context clarifies which meaning.
**식사** (siksa) = meal (more formal Hanja compound: **식** 食 + **사** 事).
**아침 식사** = breakfast (formal); **아침** alone is common in speech.
**일곱 시에** = 'at seven o'clock' — **에** marks a specific point in time.

**점심** (jeomsim) = lunch — Hanja: **점** (點, dot/noon) + **심** (心, heart/center).
Think of it as 'the center of the day'.
**두 시에** = 'at two o'clock' — Sino-Korean numbers (두 = two) are used for clock times.
**먹다** = to eat; **을** = object marker after a consonant.

**저녁** (jeonyeok) = evening AND dinner — context clarifies.
**저녁 식사** = dinner (formal); **저녁** alone is typical in casual speech.
**준비됐어요** = 'is ready' — **준비** (preparation) + **되다** (to become) in past tense, a passive-feeling construction.

**간식** (gansik) = snack — Hanja: **간** (間, between/interval) + **식** (食, food).
Literally 'between-meal food' — food eaten between main meals.
**오후에** = 'in the afternoon' — **에** marks a point in time.
cards.englishPhrase: I have a snack in the afternoon.

cards.grammarNote:
**식사** (siksa) = meal — Hanja: **식** (食, eat/food) + **사** (事, matter/affair).
**밥** (bap) is the casual everyday word for 'a meal' (also means cooked rice).
**식사하다** = to have a meal (formal verb).
**이** here = demonstrative 'this'; the topic marker **는** follows the noun **식사**.

**레시피** (resipi) is a loanword from English.
Native term: **조리법** (jorippop) = cooking method/recipe.
**좋은 레시피 있어요?** — dropping the subject and topic marker makes this a natural, casual question: 'do you have a good recipe?'
cards.englishPhrase: Do you have a good recipe?

cards.grammarNote:
**맛** (mat) = taste, flavor — the root of **맛있다** (delicious) and **맛없다** (tasteless/bland).
Flavor words: **단맛** (sweet taste), **신맛** (sour taste), **쓴맛** (bitter taste), **짠맛** (salty taste), **매운맛** (spicy taste).
**어떤** (eotteon) = 'what kind of'.

**재료** (jaeryo) = ingredient, material, raw material.
**어떤 재료** = 'what ingredients' — **어떤** = 'what kind of / which'.
**필요하다** (piryohada) = to need / to be necessary.
**가** = subject marker after a vowel: **재료가 필요해요** = 'ingredients are needed'.

**메뉴** (menyu) is a loanword from French/English.
In Korean restaurants, menus often have pictures — very helpful for ordering.
**볼 수 있어요?** = 'can I see?' — **V-ㄹ 수 있다** = 'can / be able to'.
**메뉴** without a particle in **메뉴 볼 수 있어요?** is informal but natural.

**식당** (sikdang) = restaurant/eatery — Hanja: **식** (食, food) + **당** (堂, hall).
**레스토랑** (reseutorang) = formal/upscale restaurant (loanword).
**이** = topic marker after a consonant: **식당은**.
The topic marker **은/는** is common when making evaluations.

**주방** (jubang) = kitchen — Hanja: **주** (廚, cook) + **방** (房, room).
**부엌** (bueok) = kitchen (native Korean word) — used interchangeably with **주방**.
**깨끗하다** (kkaekkeuthada) = to be clean — a descriptive verb that acts as its own predicate.
**이** = subject marker after a consonant: **주방이**.

**오븐** (obeun) is a loanword.
Ovens are less common in Korean homes than in Western kitchens — **가스레인지** (gas range/stove) and **전자레인지** (microwave) are more typical.
**뜨겁다** (tteugeopda) = hot to the touch (temperature) — not to be confused with **맵다** (spicy).

**가스레인지** (gaseu reinji) = gas stove/range — a loanword compound from 'gas range'.
**에서** after a location = where an action takes place: **가스레인지에서** = 'on the stove'.
**요리하다** is a **하다** verb: **요리** (cooking) + **하다** (to do).

**칼** (kal) = knife — also appears in **칼국수** (kalguksu, knife-cut noodles).
**잘 드는 칼** = 'a sharp knife': **잘** (well) + **들다** (to enter/go in) — a knife that 'cuts well'.
**필요하다** (piryohada) = 'to need / to be necessary'.

**포크** (pokeu) is a loanword.
Traditional Korean utensils: **젓가락** (chopsticks) and **숟가락** (spoon).
Forks are provided at most restaurants today, especially for non-Korean food.
**주실 수 있어요?** = 'can you give me?' — **주시다** is the honorific of **주다** + **-ㄹ 수 있다** (can).

**숟가락** (sutgarak) = spoon — native Korean.
In Korean dining etiquette, you hold the spoon in your right hand for soup and rice, and use **젓가락** (chopsticks) for side dishes.
**필요해요** = 'is needed' — from **필요하다** used as a predicate.

**접시** (jeopsi) = plate — native Korean.
**더럽다** (deoreopda) = to be dirty — irregular ㅂ verb: **더러워요** in speech.
Opposite: **깨끗하다** (to be clean).
**가** = subject marker after a vowel: **접시가**.

**그릇** (geureus) = bowl/vessel — a general word for eating and cooking vessels.
Specific compounds: **밥그릇** (rice bowl), **국그릇** (soup bowl).
**에** = 'into/in': **그릇에** = 'into the bowl'.
**담다** (damda) = to put/fill — used for loading food into a container.

**유리잔** (yurijan) = drinking glass: **유리** (glass material) + **잔** (cup/glass).
**잔** is both a counter (for glasses of liquid) and a noun meaning 'cup/glass'.
**한 잔** = one glass — **한** is the contracted form of **하나** (one) before a counter.

**병** (byeong) has two meanings: 'bottle' (瓶) and 'illness/sickness' (病) — same sound, different Hanja.
Context always makes the meaning clear.
**병** as a counter: **한 병** (one bottle), **두 병** (two bottles).
For illness: **병이 났어요** = 'I got sick'.

**테이블** (teibeul) is a loanword — the Western-style table.
**상** (sang) = traditional low Korean dining table (floor-seating style).
**저희** (jeohui) = 'we/our' — the humble form of **우리** (uri), used to show modesty.
**준비됐어요** = 'is ready' — **준비** (preparation) + **되다** (to become).

**웨이터** (weiteo) is a loanword, used mostly for upscale restaurants.
In everyday Korean restaurants, address staff as **저기요!** (jeogiyo, 'excuse me!') — more natural.
**직원** (jigwon) = staff/employee — a neutral term for all service workers.
**계산서** (gyesanseo) = bill/check.

**배고프다** (stomach is empty) → **배고파요** in speech.
**목마르다** (throat is dry) → **목말라요** in speech.
Both are adjectives in Korean, not verbs.

**목마르다** (mongmareuda) = to be thirsty — literally 'the throat is dry': **목** (throat/neck) + **마르다** (to dry/wither).
The stem **마르** is irregular — conjugates to **말라요**: **목말라요**.
For hunger: **배고프다** (baegoppeuda) — literally 'the stomach is empty'.

**먹다** (meokda) = to eat — one of the most common Korean verbs.
Polite form: **먹어요** (meogeoyo).
Honorific: **드시다** (deusida) — for eating by elders/superiors: **드세요** (please eat).
Before meals: **잘 먹겠습니다** (jal meokgesseumnida, 'I will eat well').
After meals: **잘 먹었습니다** (jal meogeossseumnida, 'I ate well / thank you for the meal').

**마시다** (masida) = to drink.
Honorific: **드시다** (deusida) covers both eating and drinking respectfully.
**마실 것** = 'something to drink': **마시다** + **-ㄹ 것** (thing to V).
**드릴까요?** = 'shall I give/offer?' — honorific of **줄까요**, very commonly heard at cafés and restaurants.
cards.englishPhrase: Would you like something to drink?

cards.grammarNote:
**요리하다** (yorihada) = to cook — a **하다** verb.
**요리** (yori) alone is the noun 'cooking/cuisine'.
**잘하다** = 'to do well': **요리를 잘해요** = 'cooks well'.
**아주** (aju) = very/extremely — slightly more emphatic than **많이** in this context.

**굽다** (gupda) = to bake, grill, or roast — used for bread, meat, and fish.
Irregular ㅂ verb: **굽다** → **구워요** (the ㅂ changes to 우 before vowels).
**일요일에** = 'on Sunday' — **에** marks a specific point or period in time.

**튀기다** (twigida) = to deep-fry.
**볶다** (bokkda) = to stir-fry — more common in Korean cooking.
The phrase uses **볶으세요** (stir-fry please) because stir-frying onions in oil is a common Korean cooking technique.
**에** = 'in': **기름에** = 'in the oil'.

**굽다** (gupda) = to roast, grill, or bake — the same verb for all dry-heat cooking.
**구우세요** = 'please roast' — the **-으세요** polite imperative after a vowel stem.
**한 시간** = one hour — note **한** (native Korean 'one') used for duration, not Sino-Korean **일** (il).
cards.englishPhrase: Roast the chicken for one hour.

cards.grammarNote:
**다지다** (dajida) = to mince or chop finely.
**썰다** (sseolda) = to slice or cut in larger pieces — more general.
**자르다** (jareuda) = to cut (general cutting).
**다지세요** = 'please chop/mince' — **-으세요** polite imperative after a consonant.

**썰다** (sseolda) = to slice/cut — the general verb for slicing food.
**얇게** = 'thinly' — the adverb form of **얇다** (to be thin).
**두껍게** = 'thickly' — opposite adverb.
**써세요** = 'please slice' — **-으세요** polite imperative.

**섞다** (seokda) = to mix/blend.
**비비다** (bibida) = to mix by rubbing/tossing — the action in **비빔밥** (bibimbap, mixed rice).
**모든** (modeun) = all/every — the modifier form of **모두** (all).
**섞으세요** = 'please mix' — **-으세요** after a consonant ending.

**젓다** (jeotda) = to stir — used for stirring liquids like soup, batter, or coffee.
**저어세요** = 'please stir' — the conjugated imperative form.
**국을** — **을** = object marker after a consonant: **국** (soup) ends in consonant ㄱ.

**거품기** (geopumgi) = whisk: **거품** (foam/bubbles) + **기** (machine/tool).
**로** = 'with / by means of (a tool)': **거품기로** = 'with a whisk'.
**저어세요** = 'please stir/whisk' — same verb **젓다** used for stirring with any implement.

**붓다** (butda) = to pour — irregular: **부어요** in polite speech (the ㅅ drops).
**에** after a container = 'into': **유리잔에** = 'into the glass'.
**부어세요** = 'please pour' — **-어세요** imperative form.
cards.englishPhrase: Pour the water into the glass.

cards.grammarNote:
**서빙하다** (seobinghada) = to serve (food) — a **하다** verb from the English loanword 'serving'.
Native Korean alternative: **내다** (naeda) or **내놓다** (naenota) = to put out/serve.
**가** = subject marker after a vowel: **웨이터가**.

**먹이다** (meogida) = to feed — the causative of **먹다** (to eat): 'to cause someone to eat'.
**에게** = 'to (a person or animal)': **아기에게** = 'to the baby'.
**먹여요** is the conjugated form of **먹이다** (irregular form).

**식사하다** (siksahada) = to have a meal/dine — formal **하다** verb.
**에서** after a place = where an action takes place: **좋은 식당에서** = 'at a nice restaurant'.
**식사했어요** = 'dined / had a meal' — past tense of **식사하다**.
cards.englishPhrase: We dined at a nice restaurant.

cards.grammarNote:
**씹다** (ssipda) = to chew.
**씹으세요** = 'please chew' — **-으세요** polite imperative after a consonant.
**천천히** (cheoncheonhi) = slowly — a very useful adverb for instructions.
**음식** (eumsik) = food — Hanja: **음** (飮) + **식** (食).

**삼키다** (samkida) = to swallow.
**과** = 'and/with' — connects nouns after a consonant: **물과** = 'with water'.
**함께** (hamkke) = together.
**약** (yak) = medicine, pill — also means a general remedy.

**간** (gan) = saltiness level / seasoning — not a spice itself, but the balance of salt.
**간을 하다** = to season (food).
**간을 보다** = to taste/check the seasoning.
**로** = 'with / by means of': **소금과 후추로** = 'with salt and pepper'.

**신선하다** (sinseonhada) = to be fresh — commonly used for food and produce.
**신선해요** = polite present form.
**갓 구운** = 'just baked': **갓** (just/freshly) + **굽다** (to bake) in past modifier form.
**이** = subject marker after a consonant: **빵이**.

**익다** (ikda) = to ripen (fruit) or to cook through (food) — the same verb for both.
**잘 익은** = 'well-ripened' — **잘** (well) + **익은** (past modifier of **익다**).
**잘 익었어요** = 'has ripened well' — past tense indicating completed change.
**가** = subject marker after a vowel: **토마토가**.

**익히다** (ikhida) = to cook through (the causative of **익다**): 'to make something ripen/cook'.
**익힌** = the past modifier: 'cooked'.
**익었어요?** = 'has it cooked through?' — uses the intransitive **익다**.
Ask this to check doneness of meat — very useful at Korean BBQ.

**뜨겁다** = hot to the touch (temperature).
**맵다** = spicy-hot.
**따뜻하다** = warm.
Never confuse **뜨겁다** with **맵다** in a food context — one is temperature, the other is spice.
cards.englishPhrase: Be careful, the soup is hot.

cards.grammarNote:
**차갑다** (chagapda) = cold to the touch (物理的 cold, like an object or drink) — irregular ㅂ: **차가워요**.
**춥다** (chupda) = cold weather or how cold a person feels: **추워요**.
**식다** (sikda) = to cool down: **식었어요** = 'has cooled / gone cold' — used for food or drinks that were hot.

**달다** (dalda) = to be sweet — the base adjective verb.
**달콤하다** (dalkomhada) = to be sweetly sweet — slightly more poetic/romantic nuance.
**단맛** (danmat) = sweet taste/flavor: **단** (sweet) + **맛** (taste).
**달아요** and **달콤해요** are both used naturally; **달콤해요** often describes pleasant, mild sweetness.

**시다** (sida) = to be sour — the base adjective verb.
**셔요** is the polite form: **이 레몬은 셔요** (this lemon is sour).
**신맛** (sinmat) = sour taste: **신** (sour) + **맛** (taste).
Related: **새콤하다** (saekomhada) = slightly tangy/sour — often used approvingly for refreshing sourness.

**쓰다** (sseuda) = to be bitter — the base adjective verb.
**써요** is the polite present form: **이 커피는 써요** (this coffee is bitter).
**쓴맛** (sseunmat) = bitter taste: **쓴** (bitter) + **맛** (taste).
**쓴** is the modifier form used before nouns: **쓴 약** (bitter medicine), **쓴 커피** (bitter coffee).

**맵다** (maepda) = to be spicy — irregular ㅂ verb: **매워요** in polite speech.
**매운맛** (maeunmat) = spicy taste.
**매운** is the modifier form before nouns: **매운 음식** (spicy food), **매운 김치** (spicy kimchi).
Do not confuse **맵다** (spicy) with **뜨겁다** (hot temperature) — they are entirely different in Korean.

**부드럽다** (budeureopda) = to be soft/tender/smooth — irregular ㅂ verb: **부드러워요**.
**부드러운** is the modifier form before nouns: **부드러운 고기** (tender meat).
**연하다** (yeonhada) = to be soft/tender specifically for food texture — common when describing well-cooked meat.
Opposite: **질기다** (jilgida) = to be tough/chewy (meat).

**맛있어요** = delicious.
Cultural meal phrases:
**잘 먹겠습니다** — said before eating ('I will eat well').
**잘 먹었습니다** — said after eating ('I ate well').
Both express gratitude to the host or cook.

**맛있다** (masitda) = to be tasty/delicious: **맛** (taste) + **있다** (to exist/have) — literally 'taste exists'.
**맛있는** is the modifier form before nouns: **맛있는 국** (tasty soup).
**맛없다** (maseopsda) = tasteless/bland — literally 'taste doesn't exist'.
**아주** and **진짜** both intensify: **아주 맛있어요** (very tasty), **진짜 맛있어요** (really tasty).

**싱겁다** (singeobda) = to be bland/under-seasoned — irregular ㅂ verb: **싱거워요**.
**싱거운** is the modifier form: **싱거운 밥** (bland rice).
**간이 없다** = 'there is no seasoning' — an alternative way to describe bland food.
Opposite: **짜다** (jjada) = to be salty/over-seasoned.

**건강하다** (geonganghada) = to be healthy — Sino-Korean: **건** (健, strong) + **강** (康, well).
**건강에 좋다** = 'good for health': **에** here means 'for'.
**건강한 음식** = healthy food — **건강한** is the modifier form.
The opposite: **건강에 나쁘다** = 'bad for health'.

**건강에 나쁘다** = 'bad for health' — literally 'bad to health'.
**나쁘다** (nappeuda) = to be bad — **에** marks what something is bad for.
**패스트푸드** (paeseutupudu) = fast food (loanword from English).
**몸에 안 좋다** is a natural alternative: **몸에 안 좋아요** = 'not good for the body'.

**채식주의자** (chaesigjuuija) = vegetarian — a compound Sino-Korean noun: **채식** (plant-based eating) + **주의** (principle/ideology) + **자** (person).
**채식** alone refers to a vegetarian diet; **채식하다** = to eat vegetarian.
At restaurants: **채식 메뉴 있어요?** (do you have vegetarian menu options?) is a practical phrase.
**비건** (bigeon) = vegan — a loanword used for stricter plant-based eating.

**비건** (bigeon) is a loanword from English 'vegan'.
**채식주의자** (chaesigjuuija) = vegetarian; **비건** = vegan (stricter, excludes all animal products).
**이에요** = copula after a consonant: **비건이에요** = 'is vegan'.
Useful at restaurants: **유제품도 안 드세요?** = 'do you also not eat dairy?'

**냉동** (naengdong) = freezing/frozen — Hanja: **냉** (冷, cold) + **동** (凍, freeze).
**냉동식품** (naengdongsikpum) = frozen food — **식품** (food product).
**냉장** (naengjang) = refrigeration (not freezing): **냉장고** (naengjanggo) = refrigerator.
**꺼내다** (kkeohaeда) = to take out — used for removing food from the fridge or freezer.

**유기농** (yuginong) = organic — Hanja: **유기** (有機, organic/living matter) + **농** (農, farming).
**유기농 채소** = organic vegetables; **유기농 식품** = organic food products.
**더 좋아해요** = 'prefer' (literally 'like more'): **더** (more) + **좋아하다** (to like).
Organic produce is labelled **유기농** in Korean supermarkets and markets.

**집에서 만든** (jibeseo mandeun) = homemade — literally 'made at home'.
**집** (jip) = home/house; **에서** = at (location of action); **만든** = past modifier of **만들다** (to make).
The same pattern works for any origin: **시장에서 산** (bought at the market), **엄마가 만든** (made by mom).
**집밥** (jipbap) = home-cooked meal — a beloved compound used affectionately.

**매일** (maeil) = every day/daily — Hanja: **매** (每, every) + **일** (日, day).
**매일** does NOT take the time particle **에** — it stands alone as a time adverb.
**매주** (maeju) = every week; **매달** (maedal) = every month; **매년** (maenyeon) = every year.
These all follow the same **매-** pattern.

**거의** (geoui) = almost/nearly — used before verbs or adjectives to mean 'nearly' a state.
**거의 다** = 'almost all done': **거의 다 됐어요** = 'it's almost ready'.
**다** (da) = all/completely — pairs with **거의** to emphasise near-completion.
**됐어요** = 'became / is done' from **되다** (to become) in past tense — a very common completion marker.

**너무** (neomu) = too much / excessively — often implies a negative degree ('it's too much').
In modern colloquial Korean **너무** is also used positively: **너무 맛있어요!** (so delicious!) — the negative implication has softened.
**너무** + adjective: **너무 짜요** (too salty), **너무 매워요** (too spicy), **너무 달아요** (too sweet).
**많다** (manta) = to be many/much — **너무 많아요** = 'there is too much'.
